sellable_entry_point.rationale: Foundation at EUR 195K for sovereign cloud + migration of 50 workloads is a surgical entry for a EUR 1B+ utility. It leads with the most acute pain -- NIS2 compliance deadline (Oct 2026) plus immediate infrastructure modernization. A CIO facing regulatory fines of up to EUR 10M will not push this to next quarter. The 14-week parallel delivery means results before the next board cycle. My AEs can pitch this in the first meeting without any pre-briefing: 'BSI C5 readiness plus your first 50 workloads migrated, EUR 195K, 14 weeks.' Done.

upgrade_narrative.rationale: The Foundation-to-Professional path is logical: once you've moved 50 workloads to sovereign cloud, the conversation about OT/IT integration and SAP IS-U migration surfaces naturally. The AE can say 'You now have BSI C5 sovereign infrastructure -- let's bring your SCADA systems into a secure hybrid architecture and migrate SAP IS-U.' The one knock: the 2.7x price jump from EUR 195K to EUR 520K is meaningful. An AE needs to frame the hybrid-cloud addition as the unlock that justifies the step-up, not just 'more of the same at bigger scale.' The scaling of sovereign and migration from small to medium alone wouldn't justify the jump -- hybrid-cloud (OT/IT air-gap architecture) is the real new-value driver and needs to be positioned that way.

deal_size_optimization.rationale: The EUR 195K to EUR 1.295M progression is strong land-and-expand territory. Enterprise tier at EUR 1.295M with dual-hyperscaler managed operations and 500+ workload migration justifies CIO or even board-level sponsorship. The split of EUR 1.1M project + EUR 195K recurring-transition is smart -- it plants the seed for ongoing managed services revenue. One concern: there is no explicit managed-services annuity quoted in the Enterprise tier. After the EUR 195K onboarding, what does the annual run rate look like? My AEs will get asked 'what does this cost per year after go-live?' in the first CxO meeting, and they need a number. That gap slightly caps the perceived long-term deal size.

competitive_resilience.rationale: This package plays directly into T-Systems' unfair advantages. Sovereign cloud with BSI C5 Type 2 attestation -- Accenture cannot deliver this, Capgemini cannot deliver this. The OT/IT hybrid architecture with air-gap SCADA isolation leverages operational depth that consulting-led competitors lack. The E.ON reference (2,500 SAP workloads migrated) is a conversation-closer in any energy CIO meeting. Every tier reinforces 'why T-Systems': Foundation = sovereign infrastructure + KRITIS compliance, Professional = OT/IT integration on DT backbone, Enterprise = 24/7 managed operations with 15-minute incident response. A competitor comparison slide writes itself.

pricing_story.rationale: The bundle economics are clean and tell a story. Standalone components for Foundation would cost EUR 230K (sovereign 110K + migration 120K); the package delivers it for EUR 195K -- a 15% saving my AEs can state in one sentence. At Professional, components sum to roughly EUR 625K vs. package price EUR 520K (17% off). At Enterprise, roughly EUR 1.6M vs. EUR 1.295M (19% off). The increasing discount at higher tiers rewards commitment -- exactly the dynamic you want in a procurement negotiation. The one gap: the package does not surface a combined proof-of-value entry below Foundation. Individual solutions have PoV options at EUR 18K-25K, but there is no 'package PoV' at say EUR 40K. When a CIO says 'I like it but EUR 195K is too much to start before I see results,' the AE has to improvise.

objection_readiness.rationale: The package handles most procurement objections well. 'Why not buy components separately?' -- 15-19% bundle savings depending on tier, clearly documented. 'Why this tier not the next?' -- each tier adds a distinct capability (hybrid, managed ops) rather than just scaling the same services. 'What if we only need sovereign cloud?' -- the solution is available standalone, but the package shows the value of combining it with migration. One gap: the exclusions list is solid but the Foundation tier excludes 'ongoing compliance monitoring after handover' -- a sharp procurement team will ask 'so who monitors compliance after week 14?' and the AE needs a ready answer about a managed-services follow-on. That handoff should be pre-scripted, not left to the AE.

top_issues.issue: No package-level proof-of-value tier below Foundation. Individual solutions offer PoVs at EUR 18K-25K, but there is no combined entry at EUR 35K-45K for a CIO who wants to validate before committing EUR 195K.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Add a 'Discovery' or 'PoV' tier that bundles the sovereign-cloud PoV (NIS2 gap assessment) with the cloud-migration PoV (5-workload pilot) at a combined EUR 40K, 4-week delivery. This gives AEs a low-friction entry when Foundation faces budget timing objections.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: packages SKILL.md tier design principles -- minimum entry tier definition and PoV bundling logic

top_issues.issue: Enterprise tier references EUR 195K 'recurring-transition' for managed-hyperscaler onboarding but does not quote the annual managed-services run rate. AEs will be asked about ongoing costs in the first CxO meeting.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Include an indicative annual managed-operations run rate in the Enterprise tier scope (e.g., 'EUR 480K-720K/year for 24/7 managed operations of up to 300 instances') or add a note referencing the managed-hyperscaler pricing model for ongoing costs.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: packages SKILL.md scope and pricing -- recurring revenue visibility in project-type packages

top_issues.issue: Foundation tier excludes 'ongoing compliance monitoring after handover' without a clear handoff to a managed service. Procurement will ask who monitors BSI C5 compliance after week 14.
top_issues.suggested_fix: Add a line in Foundation scope or a cross-reference: 'Compliance monitoring available as add-on via Sovereign Cloud managed service (from EUR X/month)' to pre-empt the question.
top_issues.root_cause_hint: packages SKILL.md exclusion handling -- managed-service follow-on linkage

strengths: Foundation tier leads with the most urgent pain point -- NIS2 compliance deadline and BSI C5 readiness -- creating genuine buyer urgency rather than aspirational interest.
Every tier reinforces T-Systems' unfair advantages (sovereign infrastructure, KRITIS depth, E.ON reference, DT backbone) that Accenture and Capgemini cannot replicate.
Bundle savings increase with tier commitment (15% -> 17% -> 19%), giving AEs a natural negotiation lever and rewarding enterprise-level commitment.

The addition of hybrid-cloud at Professional and managed-hyperscaler at Enterprise introduces distinct new capabilities at each step, avoiding the 'same thing but bigger' trap that kills upgrade conversations.
overall_assessment: This package is field-ready. My AEs can walk into any German energy utility CIO meeting and present a clear story: start with sovereign cloud and your first 50 workloads for EUR 195K, expand to OT/IT hybrid integration at EUR 520K when SCADA modernization hits the agenda, and go enterprise-wide with managed hyperscaler operations at EUR 1.295M when the board signs off on full cloud transformation. The competitive positioning is airtight -- no competitor can match sovereign cloud with BSI C5 Type 2 plus the E.ON migration reference in the same package. Two things would make it stronger for my team: a sub-EUR 50K proof-of-value bundle for CIOs who need to validate before committing to Foundation, and an indicative annual run rate for managed operations in the Enterprise tier so AEs don't get caught flat-footed on the 'what does this cost per year' question. But as-is, I would deploy this to my top 10 energy utility accounts this quarter.
